an3.53:1.1: Then a certain brahmin approached the *Bhagavā*, and having approached, exchanged greetings with the *Bhagavā* … Seated to one side, that brahmin said this to the *Bhagavā*:
an3.53:1.2: "'The *dhamma* is directly visible, the *dhamma* is directly visible,' so it is said, Master Gotama.
an3.53:1.3: In what way, Master Gotama, is the *dhamma* directly visible, immediate, of the nature 'come and see,' leading onward, to be personally experienced by the wise?"
an3.53:2.1: "One inflamed by passion, brahmin, overcome by passion, with mind [*citta*] consumed, intends harm to themselves, intends harm to others, intends harm to both, and experiences mental *dukkha* and grief.
an3.53:2.2: With passion abandoned, they neither intend harm to themselves, nor intend harm to others, nor intend harm to both, nor experience mental *dukkha* and grief.
an3.53:2.3: One inflamed … conducts themselves in wrong conduct by body, conducts themselves in wrong conduct by speech, conducts themselves in wrong conduct by mind.
an3.53:2.4: With passion abandoned, they neither conduct themselves in wrong conduct by body, nor conduct themselves in wrong conduct by speech, nor conduct themselves in wrong conduct by mind.
an3.53:2.5: One inflamed … does not know their own good as it truly is, does not know the good of others as it truly is, does not know the good of both as it truly is.
an3.53:2.6: With passion abandoned, they know their own good as it truly is, know the good of others as it truly is, know the good of both as it truly is.
an3.53:2.7: In this way too, brahmin, the *dhamma* is directly visible ….
an3.53:3.1: One corrupted by hatred, brahmin, overcome by hatred, with mind [*citta*] consumed, intends harm to themselves, intends harm to others, intends harm to both, and experiences mental *dukkha* and grief.
an3.53:3.2: With hatred abandoned, they neither intend harm to themselves, nor intend harm to others, nor intend harm to both, nor experience mental *dukkha* and grief.
an3.53:3.3: In this way too, brahmin, the *dhamma* is directly visible ….
an3.53:4.1: One deluded by delusion, brahmin, overcome by delusion, with mind [*citta*] consumed, intends harm to themselves, intends harm to others, intends harm to both, and experiences mental *dukkha* and grief.
an3.53:4.2: With delusion abandoned, they neither intend harm to themselves, nor intend harm to others, nor intend harm to both, nor experience mental *dukkha* and grief.
an3.53:4.3: In this way, brahmin, the *dhamma* is directly visible, immediate, of the nature 'come and see,' leading onward, to be personally experienced by the wise."
an3.53:5.2: Just as, Master Gotama, one might set upright what has been overturned, or reveal what is hidden, or point out the path to one who is lost, or hold a lamp in the darkness so that those with eyes can see forms — in just the same way the *dhamma* has been made clear by Master Gotama in countless ways.
an3.53:5.3: I go for refuge to Master Gotama, to the *dhamma*, and to the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus*.
an3.53:5.4: May Master Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge from this day forward for as long as life lasts."
